This adds the dimension of height to surround sound for truly three-dimensional audio. While 7.1.2 beds are sufficient for most channel- based mixing needs, some plugins, such as reverbs and delays, may want to take full advantage of the ceiling space. Pro Tools supports channel configurations with additional height speakers, such as 5.0.4 to 9.1.6. While these cannot be sent directly to an external renderer, it is possible to use statically panned 60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos objects to send the additional height speakers to the Dolby Atmos renderer. Depending on your system configuration, the internal renderer supports up to 9.1.6 monitoring. Pro Tools provides track presets to aid with these configurations.
All height channels in immersive surround formats have the channel positions against the edges of the room to take full advantage of the three dimensional space. Sound is heard immediately if panned inward on the ceiling. Note that 7.1.2 height channels have a slightly different pan law, as specified by Dolby for use with beds feeding the Dolby Atmos renderer (internal or external). Signal on the ceiling does not pan into the middle of the room until you have reached a quarter of the distance inward.
Mixing to Dolby Atmos with Pro Tools Ultimate or Studio Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio both provide the following features supporting mixing to a Dolby Atmos renderer (internal or external):
Up to 7.1.2 channel width for direct routing of beds to the Dolby Atmos renderer.
- Up to 7.1.6 (Pro Tools Studio) and up to 9.1.6 (Pro Tools Ultimate) for advanced mixing configurations
- being sent to the Dolby Atmos renderer, as well as monitoring returns and re-renders.
Mapping to dedicated Dolby Atmos Renderer Inputs in the I/O Setup.
- Object view for mono and stereo audio, Auxiliary Input, Routing Folders, and Instrument tracks.
- Automated switching between assigned track Output and an Object output path for mono and stereo
- audio, Auxiliary Input, and Instrument tracks.
- Object panning of mono and stereo sources.
- 3D “Theater” panning view.
- Enhanced object re-recording (dubbing) workflows.
g Dolby provides both hardware and software solutions for rendering and mastering a Dolby Atmos mix.
You can run the Dolby Atmos Renderer on the same machine as Pro Tools (Mac only), or on a separate machine. Contact Dolby for complete information about Dolby Atmos product solutions.
Pro Tools Restrictions when Mixing to Dolby Atmos When you create a Pro Tools session for Dolby Atmos workflows, the following restrictions apply:
Use 48 kHz or 96 kHz Session Sample Rates If working with different sample rates during sound creation or premixing, convert the session sample rate to 48 kHz or 96 kHz before working with a Dolby Atmos renderer.
Only Use a Supported Session Timecode Rate For home theater workflows, the Dolby Atmos renderer supports mixing and creating masters at the following timecode rates: 23.976, 24, 25, 29.97, 29.97 drop frame, and 30 frames per second (fps).
Set the Hardware Buffer Size to 1,024 Samples (Recommended) Sessions used to author Dolby Atmos mixes tend to be very large and processor intensive. Using the highest available Hardware Buffer Size setting is recommended for optimal performance and reliability on your Pro Tools system.
Enabling the Internal Renderer Pro Tools provides an internal Dolby Atmos renderer. Enable the Internal Renderer and configure I/O Setup to access the Dolby Atmos Renderer window.
The internal renderer is not supported with the HDX Classic Playback Engine.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos To enable the internal renderer:
Setup > I/O Dolby Atmos
1. Choose and click the tab.
Internal Renderer.
2. Enable and select
I/O Setup, Dolby Atmos: selecting Internal Renderer
3. Click Default to create the default Bed and Objects (mono or stereo).
4. Configure the rest of the I/O Setup as desired.
5. Click OK.
> **Note:** See "Dolby Atmos Renderer Window" on page 1687.
Enabling the External Renderer The Dolby Atmos page in the I/O Setup lets you configure the network connections required for sending or remote on the network.
To setup the network connection between Pro Tools and an external Dolby Atmos renderer:
1. Choose Setup > I/O.
2. Click the Dolby Atmos tab.
3. Enable and select External Renderer.
I/O Setup, Dolby Atmos: selecting External Renderer
4. Specify the Dolby Atmos Renderer host in the Dolby Atmos Renderer Host field by doing one of
the following (if there is more than one Dolby Atmos renderer on the network, make sure you specify the correct one for the Pro Tools system):
- Enter the IP Address for the Dolby Atmos renderer on the network with an audio connection to
your Pro Tools system.
- Click the Dolby Atmos Renderer Host drop-down selector to see a list of Dolby Atmos
renderers (or other Dolby Atmos rendering and mastering workstations) identified on the 60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos network and select one that has an audio connection to your Pro Tools system.
- If your Pro Tools machine is using a software Dolby Atmos renderer locally, type localhost
or select it from the drop-down selector.
Connection Status The indicator lights solid if the connection is successful. It flashes on and off if the connection is not successful. You can still work on the session even if not connected to the Dolby Atmos renderer, but Enable must be selected.
5. If desired, enable Link Object and Audio Rendering in the Preferences (see "Mixing
Preferences" on page 155).
6. Click OK to save your changes and close the I/O Setup dialog.
Reasons to Use an External Renderer The decision to use the built-in Dolby Atmos renderer as opposed to the Dolby Atmos Renderer application is purely dependent on your workflow needs. To help you decide what is right for you, here are some reasons why you still may require the external Dolby Atmos renderer:
- Dolby Atmos Music Panner support
- Support for larger post-production workflows like dubbing using multi-system configurations and
object metadata recording
- Recording and punching into a .atmos file
- Export of MP4 files
- Built-in room correction tools
- Additional speaker configurations (beyond 9.1.6, 7.1.4, 5.1.4, 5.1.2, 7.1, 5.1, 2.0, Binaural)
- Speaker array mode
- Ability to monitor live re-renders beyond what is currently supported by the internal renderer.
Configuring Pro Tools Using Core Audio with the Dolby Atmos Ren- derer (macOS only) Pro Tools supports up to 130 outputs with Core Audio when used in conjunction with the channels with Pro Tools Ultimate).
32 channels).
Using Core Audio Outputs with Pro Tools and Dolby Atmos Renderer To configure Dolby Atmos renderer and Pro Tools with increased output channel counts:
1. Install the Dolby Atmos Renderer, version 3 or higher.
2. Launch the Dolby Atmos Renderer.app.
3. Open the Dolby Atmos Renderer Preferences.
Audio Driver Core Audio.
4. Set the to
Audio Input Dolby Audio Bridge
5. Set the to (Mac only).
Audio Output
6. Set the to the device that you will use for monitoring (which can be HDX using Core
Audio, or any other Core Audio device).
7. Confirm and close the Preferences.
8. Leave the Dolby Atmos Renderer application running.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos
9. Launch Pro Tools.
10. Choose Setup > Playback Engine.
11. For Playback Engine, select Dolby Audio Bridge.
12. The recommended Hardware Buffer Size is dependent on the session sample rate: 1,024
samples for a 48 kHz session or 2,048 samples for a 96 kHz session.
OK
13. Click to save your changes and close the Playback Engine dialog.
Setup > I/O Dolby Atmos
14. Choose and click the tab.
External Renderer.
15. Enable and select
localhost
16. Connect to the Dolby Atmos Renderer application, type or select it from the drop-down
selector.
17. Configure the rest of the I/O Setup as desired.
18. Click OK.
The Dolby Atmos Renderer and Pro Tools are now configured to use up to 130 output channels with Core Audio.
Pro Tools lets you create sub-paths for Dolby Atmos beds (objects are only stereo or mono and do not support custom subpaths).
To create subpaths for a Dobly Atmos bed:
1. On the Dolby Atmos page of the I/O Setup, select the Dolby Atmos bed for which you want to create
sub-paths.
New Sub-Path.
2. Click
Create.
3. Configure the New Sub-Paths dialog as desired and click
Configuring Session Setup for Dolby Atmos To configure the Session Setup for Dolby Atmos:
1. Choose Setup > Session.
2. In the Format section, set Timecode Rate to the appropriate supported Timecode rate for mixing:
- For home theater workflows, the Dolby Atmos renderer supports mixing and creating masters
at the following timecode rates: 23.976, 24, 25, 29.97, 29.97 drop frame, and 30 frames per second (fps).
- For cinema workflows using the RMU v1.6x, the Dolby Atmos renderer supports creating
masters at 24 fps only.
3. If mixing with a 24 fps session, in the Timecode Settings section, do the following:
Audio Rate Pull Up/Down None.
a. Set to Video Rate Pull Up/Down None.
b. Set to Configuring the I/O Setup for Dolby Atmos Configure the I/O Setup according to the needs of where and how your Pro Tools system fits into the larger 60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos
> **Note:** For additional information about configuring and using Pro Tools with the Dolby Atmos renderer, see
the Dolby documentation included with your Dolby Atmos Renderer software.
Output Page Configure the Output page of the I/O Setup for routing beds and objects to an external renderer, for monitoring the output of the internal renderer, or for monitoring returns from an external renderer.
I/O Setup, Output page configured for playback to a Dolby Atmos Renderer Manually Creating and Configuring Dolby Atmos Paths for External Renderer Workflows To manually create and configure a custom Dolby Atmos path:
1. In the Output page, create outputs for Beds and Objects that match the Input Configuration of the
connected renderer. In the New Path dialog, disable the Auto-create related bus paths option to prevent buses from being created in the Bus page.
2. Create a matching set of Bed and Object paths in the Dolby Atmos page, and map the newly created
paths to the corresponding output paths.
b Option-click (Mac) or Alt-click (Windows) a checkbox in the Mapping to Output column to automatically map all matching paths at once.
3. Click OK to save your changes and close the I/O Setup dialog.
g If desired, enable Link Object and Audio Rendering in the Preferences (see "Mixing Preferences" on page 155).
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Configure the page of the I/O Setup for Dolby Atmos mixing and monitoring using the Internal Renderer or an External Renderer.
I/O Setup, Dolby Atmos page Column Displays and Controls The columns on the Dolby Atmos page provide both controls and displays for each of the rows of Bed and External Renderer Object paths. Note that when is selected, there is also a column for Mapping to Outputs (which is also available in the Bus page of the I/O Setup).
Color Displays the color for the bed or object path. Click to select a color for the path from the color palette. Path colors follow the color of any assigned Dolby Atmos group if not otherwise specified.
Path Enable/Disable Click to select (or deselect) and enable (or disable) a path.
Name Displays the path name and provides the input description that is added to an exported ADM file. Double- click to rename the path. Beds and Objects that are used in the session are in bold. Path names can be displayed as text on the Object indicators in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window (see "Show Options" on page 1692).
Type Displays Bed or Object according to the path type.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Format Displays the channel width of the path (which is defined upon path creation).
Group Displays the currently selected group name, if any, and lets you select from existing groups (see "Bed/Object Groups" on page 1665).
Binaural Mode Binaural icons are shown in the Internal Dolby Atmos tab of I/O Setup (Internal Renderer only). Select from (Off, Near, Mid, Far) the various Binaural distance model settings and to apply varying levels of spatialization to each of the bed or object channels. For more information, see "Binaural Render Mode" on page 1706.
Binaural Mode is only available when using the Internal Renderer. It does not apply to any connected External Renderer nor is it used when bouncing an ADM or re-renders when using an External Renderer.
Renderer ID ID. Each object must have a unique ID. Beds, however, can share an ID or have an independent ID.
In most cases it is recommended to use the automatically assigned ID, which is uniquely assigned upon path creation. There are times, such as after importing session data, where you must manually manage the IDs due to conflicts from the incoming paths. For more advanced workflows you may want to overlap the bed IDs to consolidate the number of channels, but still allow different groups to be assigned to different beds. This provides more flexibility when using group assignments to select which elements of the mix are included with bounced re-renders.
Mapping to Output (External Renderer Only) Displays the selected Output mapping for each Bed and Object path. Lets you select an available Output path mapped to the external renderer for each Bed and Object.
g In earlier versions of Pro Tools, Mapping to Output for Dolby Atmos was on the Bus page. Note that previously mapped buses remain mapped when you open with a legacy session.
Internal/External Renderer Controls On the Dolby Atmos page, controls for selecting and configuring the renderer vary depending on whether Internal Renderer or External Renderer is selected.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Enable Renderer Select (or deselect) to enable (or disable) the specified renderer (Internal or External). This option must be selected to mix to Dolby Atmos regardless of whether you are connected to a renderer.
Internal/External Renderer Select Internal Renderer or External Renderer. When External Renderer is selected, additional renderer configuration options are displayed.
When switching between an external and the internal renderer, any outputs mapped to external renderer paths are automatically de-activated. Then when switching back to Internal mode, these outputs are re- activated. You need to manually disable any overlapping paths before closing I/O Setup.
Renderer Host (External Renderer Only) Enter the IP Address for the External Dolby Atmos Renderer on the network that you want to use or select it from the from the drop-down list. Enter localhost if you are mixing to the Dolby Atmos Renderer application locally.
Connection Status (External Renderer Only) When Pro Tools is successfully connected to an external Dolby Atmos renderer, the Connection Status indicator lights solid. This indicator flashes on and off when Dolby Atmos is enabled, but the connection fails and Pro Tools is unable to communicate with the renderer. If Dolby Atmos is not enabled, the Connection Status indicator remains unlit.
Default (Internal Renderer Only) Click the Default button to create the default Bed and Object paths.
Create Mono or Stereo Objects Select either Create Mono Objects or Create Stereo Objects to create new mono or stereo object paths in the Dolby Atmos page of the I/O Setup. When clicking Default, all new Object paths will be created as either mono or stereo accordingly.
Pull Config (External Renderer Only) Pull Config takes the place of the Default button when External Renderer is selected. Click the Pull Config button to have Pro Tools check the currently connected renderer and create beds and objects that resemble the Input Configuration of the Dolby Atmos Renderer application.
Binaural and group assignments are pulled over for each of the paths, and the Input descriptions are used for the path names. Pulling the configuration from the renderer also creates any needed Output paths and automatically maps them to the beds and objects in the Dolby Atmos tab.
External Trim and Downmix information is also pulled over and stored even though it is not utilized when Renderer is selected. This information is available in the Trim and Downmix window if you switch to the internal renderer.
g If there is an active connection to the Dolby Atmos Renderer application, enabling Dolby Audio Bridge as an Aux IO output device will automatically pull from the renderer and create the necessary output paths directly under the Dolby Audio Bridge I/O.
Create Stereo/Mono Objects The Dolby Atmos Renderer application does not have a notion of stereo objects. Select either Create Mono Objects or Create Stereo Objects to create new mono or stereo object paths. Pro Tools creates the paths in the Dolby Atmos page. When Stereo is selected, pulling the configuration groups any objects with consecutive IDs in stereo pairs where possible.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos If you have a custom set of mono and stereo objects, it is recommended that you create all necessary Dolby Atmos and Output paths manually.
Live Re-Render Paths (Internal Renderer Only) Re-renders are copies of the mix, in addition to your main monitoring. In some cases, they may be generated using a different format, or contain only a subset of the sound elements. Pro Tools supports two different kinds of re-renders, offline and live.
Offline re-renders are available when using the internal or external renderer. They are used and configurable from the Bounce Mix window (see "Bounce Mix, Metadata, and ADM/Re-render Files" on page 1718).
Live re-renders in Pro Tools are only available when using the internal renderer. They are configurable from I/O Setup or from the Dolby Atmos Renderer window. Two simultaneous stock live re-renders are available for Loudness metering and Headphone monitoring respectively. Custom live re-renders can be configured separately. Live re-renders appear in a 're-renders' submenu on the input of tracks with a compatible width.
g Live re-renders are copies of the main monitor mix and are affected by the mute buttons in the Dolby Atmos Renderer Window.
Live Custom Re-renders Unlimited custom re-renders can be configured with specific formats and contents (see "Stock and Custom Re-Renders" on page 1715).
Live Loudness Re-render Loudness is calculated from the 5.1 copy of the mix only, not the stereo copy. Loudness is calculated from the 5.1 copy of the mix only, not the stereo copy. You can use a third-party loudness metering plugin, or use Disabled) this re-render to print the downmix in your session. Select the desired path format (or for loudness metering of the live re-render: 2.0 Direct, 2.0, or 5.1. This path can be assigned to the input of a track where you can use a loudness metering plugin.
Live Headphones Re-render Provides a Binaural, 2.0 Direct, or 2.0 copy of the mix. Route to headphones from an output that is different from your main monitors. Select either Binaural, 2.0 Direct, or 2.0 (or Disabled) for headphone monitoring.
If your main monitor is set to Binaural, your headphone re-renders must also be set to Binaural.
Groups Click the Groups button to open the Groups dialog (see "Bed/Object Groups" on the next page). Groups are used to tag elements of the mix. In post-production, they are often used to assign beds and objects to the various stems by category (dialog, music, effects, and so on), but you can use them for any creative purpose you want. Groups can be used when bouncing re-renders, letting you create mixes that only contain elements that are tagged with the specific Group assignments.
Trim and Downmix Trim and Downmix (Internal Renderer Only) Click the button to open the Trim and Downmix dialog (see "Trim and Downmix" on page 1701). Note that the text on this button lights orange if the currently open session has different settings than your New Session Defaults.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Bed/Object Groups The Dolby Atmos renderer and Pro Tools both let you create, edit, and manage Bed/Object Groups. This is useful for categorizing beds and objects into different groups (such as FX or Foley) that can be used for creating re-renders in the Dolby Atmos renderer. You can assign individual bed and object buses to Bed/Object Groups.
Groups are often used to derive re-render mixes with the Dolby Atmos renderer. You can create specific renderings of various groups to a designated channel width. For example, you can render out Music and FX groups to a 5.1 mix directly from the Dolby Atmos renderer using groups as a filtering mechanism.
Additionally, group names can be used as prefixes or suffixes on track names in Pro Tools when re- importing an ADM file. This can serve a great organizational cue to orient yourself with a fresh ADM import.
g Note that the Dolby Atmos RMU does not send its group list to Pro Tools like the Dolby Atmos Renderer application does. If you want to add group information into an ADM file when working with the Dolby Atmos RMU, manage groups with Pro Tools and create/assign the Bed/Object groups you need.
Additionally, bed descriptions are not carried over into Pro Tools or the resulting ADM file when working with the Dolby Atmos RMU, but object descriptions are. Both are carried over when using the Dolby Atmos Renderer application.
Bed/Object Group Management Pro Tools manages a list of Groups that you can define. When using an External Renderer, additions or changes to these groups are not reflected in the renderer, but you can add them to the Pro Tools list when pulling the configuration. The Pro Tools Group list is used for generating re-renders, and it is also included in any resulting ADM files.
To manage Bed/Object Groups in Pro Tools:
(Setup > I/O) Dolby Atmos
1. Open the I/O Setup and click the tab.
2. Do one of the following:
- Groups
Click the button.
- Bed/Object Group Manage
In the column, click the selector in any row and choose Bed/Object Groups.
I/O Setup, Dolby Atmos page, selecting Manage Bed/Object Groups
3. Pro Tools does not start with any Groups, but you can click Add Default to add a default set:
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos
  - Dialog
  - Music
  - Effects
  - Narration
  - Backgrounds
  - Foley
Manage Bed/Object Groups dialog
4. If you are using the internal renderer, you can do any of the following:
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos
  - Type a name for a New Group and click the “+” button or press enter to add it to the Groups
list.
  - Click the name of a group in the list and type to rename it.
  - Click the “X” to the right of the name of a group in the list to delete it.
  - Color code groups.
5. Do either of the following:
- Cancel
Click to close the dialog without making any changes.
- OK
Click to close the dialog and save your changes.
Bed/Object Group Assignments Use the Group column in the Dolby Atmos Tab to define groups. When bouncing to ADM BWF, group assignments currently reflected in the Pro Tools I/O Setup are included in the file.
To assign a Bed or Object bus to a group:
1. Open the I/O Setup (Setup > I/O) and click the Dolby Atmos tab.
2. In the Group column, click the selector for the bus you want to assign.
3. Select the desired group name (such as FX). To remove a bus from a group, select no group.
Groups Color Coding The Manage Bed/Object Groups dialog includes the option to color code Dolby Atmos Groups. Dolby Atmos Bed and Object paths inherit the assigned Group color unless you have uniquely assigned a color to a Bed or Object path.
To open the Manage Bed/Object Groups dialog:
1. Choose and click the tab.
Setup > I/O Dolby Atmos
2. Click the button.
Groups g The Groups list is empty by default, but you can add the default post-production set by clicking the Add Default button, or type a New Group Name and click the Plus (+) button to add custom groups one at a time.
To assign a color to a group:
1. In the Manage Bed/Object Groups dialog, click the Color icon to the left of the group name in the list.
2. From the resulting Color Palette, click to select the desired color. Bed and object paths inherit the
Group color if the path does not already contain an explicit color assignment.
3. Click OK.
Clear Unused Dolby Atmos Groups Pro Tools lets you clear unused Dolby Atmos Groups in a session. This is useful for maintaining a manageable number of groups after importing other sessions in shared workflow environments.
To clear unused Dolby Atmos Groups in a session:
1. Open the Dolby Atmos Groups dialog.
2. Click Clear Unused.
3. Click OK.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Mixing Bed Material While you can directly output the bed mix in the renderer (internal or external), you can also mix bed material for a Dolby Atmos mix to 7.1.2. Bus record the bed mix to a 7.1.2 audio track in preparation for playback and mixing to a Dolby Atmos renderer.
Example Bed mix 60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos To configure a Pro Tools session for mixing bed material (7.1.2 example):
1. In the Output page of the I/O Setup, create a 7.1.2 Output path assigned to the first ten physical
outputs of your audio interface (for monitoring the mix with a 7.1.2 speaker configuration).
2. In the Bus page of the I/O setup, create a 7.1.2 bus and map it to the 7.1.2 output (“Bed Monitor”).
3. Then map that 7.1.2 output bus to the first 10 inputs of the Dolby Atmos renderer.
4. Create another 7.1.2 bus with the Auto-create sub-paths option enabled (“Bed Mix”).
5. Create a new 7.1.2 audio track.
6. Assign the 7.1.2 track Output to the 7.1.2 Bed output path (“Bed Monitor”).
7. Assign the 7.1.2 track Input to the 7.1.2 internal bus (“Bed Mix”).
8. Create as many new tracks as needed for source audio.
9. Assign the track Output for each source track to the 7.1.2 internal bus (“Bed Mix”).
10. Enable Input monitor on the 7.1.2 audio track.
11. Mix the audio on the source tracks to the 7.1.2 audio track using volume and pan automation.
Object Names and Properties Object descriptions defined on the Dolby Atmos renderer are displayed in the Dolby Atmos page after pulling the input configuration.
Track Object Output Assignment After you have created Objects in the Dolby Atmos page of the I/O Setup, you can assign any single mono (or stereo) audio, Auxiliary Input, or Instrument track to a unique object output path.
To assign a new track to an object output path:
1. Create a new mono (or stereo) audio, Auxiliary Input, or Instrument track.
2. Show Object view in the Edit or Mix window (see "Object View in the Edit and Mix Windows" on
page 1671).
3. From the Object Output Path selector in Object view, select the desired available object output path
(see "Object Path Selector" on page 1673).
Object output paths are only available if they match the track channel width (mono or stereo).
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Selecting an object output path for a mono audio track Toggle the track to Object to bypass the track Output entirely and send track audio directly to the Dolby Atmos renderer (see "Bus/Object Toggle" on page 1673).
Bus Output When a track is toggled to Bus, track audio follows the selected Output path (in I/O view) but, for example, a 7.1.2 object panner appears in the track Output window, as well as in the track I/O view in the Edit and Mix windows. The channel width of the selected Output path persists, but its panning is now controlled by the 7.1.2 object panner.
Object Output Audio track set to Object Object, When a track is toggled to track audio follows the selected object output path (in Object view) and the track Output (in I/O view) is completely bypassed. The track audio follows the assigned object output path.
The Object panner appears in the track Output window, as well as in the track I/O view in the Edit and Mix windows. All pan controls are available and automatable, but no panning is applied to the track audio in Pro Tools. Rather, Pro Tools converts pan automation to Dolby Atmos metadata, which is sent to the Dolby Atmos renderer.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Object View in the Edit and Mix Windows Edit and Mix Window Object view provides Object controls for audio, Auxiliary Input, and Instrument tracks I/O Real- (mono and stereo only). In the Edit window, these controls appear between the column and the Time Properties column of all Track and Ruler views. In the Mix window, these controls appear between the I/O selectors and the Auto selector. Object view can be shown or hidden independently in both the Edit and Mix windows.
To show (or hide) the Edit Window Object view, do one of the following:
- Select (or deselect) View > Edit Window Views > Object.
- From the Edit Window View selector, enable (or disable) Object.
Object Track view, Edit window To show (or hide) the Object controls in the Mix window, do one of the following:
- View > Mix Window Views > Object.
Select (or deselect)
- Object.
From the Mix Window View selector, enable (or disable) Object view, Mix window Object Controls Object controls shown in the Edit and Mix window Object view mirror each other on a track-by-track basis.
These controls let you assign audio, Auxiliary Input, and Instrument tracks to object output paths, automate toggling of the track between the Bus and Object output, and set the control mode for object panning metadata. Object controls when using an External renderer are limited compared to when using the Internal renderer.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Object controls in the Mix window, Internal renderer Object controls in the Mix window, External renderer Object controls in the Edit window, Internal renderer 60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Object controls in the Edit window, External renderer Object Path Selector The Object Path Selector lets you assign the track to any available object output path (defined in the Bus page of the I/O Setup).
Selecting an object output path for a mono audio track Output Window Button The Output Window button in the Object view opens the Output Window for the track.
Bus/Object Toggle The Bus/Object toggle switches the routing of track audio between the Output selected in the I/O view (Bus) (Object).
and the selected object output path
- When toggled to Bus, the track behaves like a normal Pro Tools track. The only exception is that
track panning is controlled by the object panner regardless of the channel width of the selected Output.
When toggled to Object, the selected Output in I/O view is completely bypassed and track audio
- follows the selected object output path to the Dolby Atmos renderer. Also, all pan automation is
translated to Dolby Atmos metadata and sent to the Dolby Atmos renderer. No panning automation is applied to the track audio in Pro Tools.
Bus/Object toggle can be automated (see "Object/Bus Toggle Automation" on page 1683).
Object Control Mode The Object Control Mode button lets you cycle between the three different Master Object Control modes:
Master, Record, and Off/Bypass. Click the Object Control Mode button to cycle through and select the Object Control mode.
Object pan metadata for a track is only included in an exported BWF ADM file if Object Control Mode is set to Master for that track.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Mode Description Purpose Master Object Set by default if no other tracks are already assigned to this assigned, object, use this mode to control an object on the Dolby Atmos sending renderer.
metadata Record Object Use this mode in a re-recording workflow to record pan assigned, automation sent from a Master panner on the network.
record mode Off/bypass Object Use this mode to mix multiple tracks of audio to the same object assigned, no output path. In this workflow, one track set as Master controls metadata the panning, while one or more other tracks with the same object being sent output path assignment but set to Off/bypass feed audio to the object output path but without sending pan metadata.
Binaural and Group Settings on Tracks Pro Tools lets you toggle between Binaural mode and Dolby Atmos Group settings on the Object section of tracks in both the Mix and Edit windows when using the Internal Renderer. Assigning binaural mode settings on tracks lets you change the mode in real-time and directly hear the affect on the mix, without having to stop and visit IO Setup. Assigning the groups on the track lets you categorize the objects or bed assigned to the track more quickly, giving you better context for the assignment. Binaural mode and Group settings are hidden from tracks in External renderer mode.
Binaural Track Settings Edit Window: Binaural mode settings 60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Mix Window: Binaural mode settings Group Track Settings Edit Window: Group settings Mix Window: Group setting This setting is dependent on the Object/Bus state since both an Object and a Bed can be assigned to a Mixed track and they might have independent settings. The selector displays if sub-paths are assigned to different settings. For example, if a stereo object is assigned to a stereo track, but its mono sub-paths are assigned to different mono tracks and each have different groups and binaural settings.
3D Panner When a mono or stereo track is assigned to a 5.0.2 to 9.1.6 output bus, or to an object output path, it uses the corresponding 3D panner.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Output window in standard (2D) view for a mono track assigned to a 7.1.2 bus (left) and an Object (right) Panner Controls and Indicators The following elements of the Pro Tools Output window are specific to the 3D surround and Object panners.
The Output window for each provides the same controls, except that the Object panner additionally provides the Object Output Path selector and the Object Control Mode button, and the x.0.x panner excludes the LFE fader.
Theater Mode The Pro Tools Panning Mode selector provides the Theater option, which is only available with the 3D surround and Object panners. When this mode is selected, the panner displays a 3D/Theater view.
Enabling Theater mode Theater mode provides a 3D room view that can be rotated.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos To rotate the room view in Theater mode:
- Move the cursor over the room view and use the scroll-wheel to rotate the room up or down. Scroll all
the way down for top-down view.
Theater mode, view rotated to “top-down” Indication of Height, Proximity, and Location in 3D/Theater Mode Height Adjusting the Height value raises or lowers the Height plane. See 1 in "Graphic representation of height, proximity, and location in Theater mode" on the next page.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Proximity The size of the pan dot indicates proximity (whether the element is closer or further away relative to the current view). The pan dot appears smallest when panned furthest away and largest when panned nearest (see 2 in "Graphic representation of height, proximity, and location in Theater mode" below).
Location Pan lines appear on the Height plane to indicate Left/Right and Front/Rear location. See 3 in "Graphic representation of height, proximity, and location in Theater mode" below.
Screen The Screen image provides a visual reference for the front room in all views. See 4 in "Graphic representation of height, proximity, and location in Theater mode" below.
Graphic representation of height, proximity, and location in Theater mode Height Mode Sets the Height mode for the track. Choices include FreeForm mode (manual height adjustment), and Wedge, Sphere, and Ceiling modes (automatic height modes). Height mode can be automated.
Height mode and Height Enable To select the Height mode:
- Click a Height mode icon to select that mode.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos To toggle Height on/off:
- Click the Height Enable icon. When lit, Height is enabled (requires FreeForm Height mode).
- Height Enable can be automated.
b Control-Click (Mac) or Start-Click (Windows) on a floor-level speaker icon to snap to the speaker location without affecting height.
Height The Height knob becomes available when FreeForm mode and Height is enabled/active. Height can be automated.
Adjusting Height in standard 2D view displays a vertical bar to the right of the pan grid to indicate relative height of that track. In addition, the size of the pan dot increases as Height is increased.
In Theater mode a colored Height plane shows the current Height value. The size of the pan dot indicates proximity (see "Indication of Height, Proximity, and Location in 3D/Theater Mode" on page 1677).
Zones The Zones selector sets the speaker Zone (Zone Mask). Choices include All, F/S (front and side), F/R (front and rear), FC/R (front, center and rear), Front, and Rear. Speaker icons surrounding the panner grid appear as appropriate for the current Zone.
For 9.x.x panners, L/R and LW/RW are part of the same zone.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Zones selector To select the Zone:
- Right-click, or click and hold on the Zones selector and set the Zone.
Zones can be automated.
Size Increases or decreases the element size. When Size is set to any value above 0/off, a transparent grid (or cube if in 3D/Theater mode) surrounds the pan dot. The grid/cube gets larger as Size is increased, and smaller as it is decreased.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Size indicator in standard 2D view To change the element Size:
- Adjust the Size knob, or hold down Shift and use the scroll wheel.
Speaker Snap You can toggle Dolby Atmos Speaker Snap on or off for tracks assigned to an Object.
To enable (or disable) Speaker Snap:
- Click the Speaker Snap icon to toggle on/off.
Speaker Snap icon (shown disabled) g Do not confuse Dolby Atmos Speaker Snap with the Pro Tools panner ability to click a speaker icon to “jump” the pan dot to a location.
Object Output Path Selector and Object Control Mode Across the top of the Output window are the Audio Output Path selector, the Object Output Path selector, and the Object Control button.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Height mode and Height Enable Available only on tracks assigned to an object output path, these controls let you assign the track to a different Object and configure its Object Control mode, respectively. These controls are also available in Object view in the Edit and Mix windows.
Object Output Path Selector Click the Object Output Path selector to assign the track to an Object (see "Object Path Selector" on page 1673).
Toggling Object/Bus Assignment Command-click (Mac) or Control-click (Windows) the Object Output Path selector or the pan dot to toggle the track audio output between the selected Object path or selected track output (output bus). Toggling between object output and bus output assignments can be automated (see "Bus/Object Toggle" on page 1673).
Object Control Click the Object Control mode button to cycle through and select the Object Control mode (see "Object Control Mode" on page 1673).
Object/Bus Assignment and Automation Mode Indication The Pan Dot control in Output windows, and in the graphic panner in I/O view on tracks in both the Edit and Mix windows changes color to show whether track audio follows the selected output bus path shown in the I/O view or the selected object output path shown in the Object view as well whether the Track Automation mode is set to Read, any Write mode, or is Off.
Pan Dot Color Indication Pan Dot Color Object/Bus Automation Mode Green Bus Read Orange Object Read Red Any Any Write mode Yellow Any Off Gray Object, but off/bypassed About Object Bypass Bypass can be used to establish a workflow where multiple source tracks are assigned to the same object but only one of them is sending pan metadata to control the panning on the Dolby Atmos renderer. For more information, see "Dolby Atmos PEC/DIR Recorder Workflow Example" on page 1707.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Object/Bus Toggle Automation The Object/Bus Toggle setting can be automated on object tracks. To be able to write Object/Bus Toggle automation for all Object tracks in the session, ensure that it is enabled in the Automation window. If you want to ensure that no Object/Bus Toggle automation is written at all, or if want to ensure that existing Object/Bus Toggle automation is not over-written, disable it in the Automation window.
Object/Bus toggle (shown disabled) in the Automation window.
Auto-Height Overrides Height Automation Option With multichannel panning automation, Pro Tools lets you re-purpose existing Front, Rear, and Front/Rear pan automation for Height automation when mixing to Dolby Atmos (7.0.2 or 7.1.2). When this option is enabled, the selected auto-height mode overrides any Height automation, generating new height automation automatically. This way you don’t have to re-write all new automation when opening existing 5.1 or 7.1 sessions and re-mixing them for Dolby Atmos (7.0.2 or 7.1.2 panning).
g The Auto-Height Overrides Height Automation option defaults to on if the track already has a main output assignment prior to assigning an object output (assuming a re-use workflow). This option defaults to off when translating automation from the Dolby Atmos Panner plugin and also when a track has an object output assigned but no main output assigned.
g To commit automatically generated height automation, use the Coalesce Pan Guide Automation command. See "Coalesce Pan Guide Automation" on the next page.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos To enable (or disable) Auto-Height Overrides Height Automation:
1. Open the Output window for the track you want.
2. Do one of the following:
- Double-click the Height Mode selector.
- Right-click the Height Mode selector and select (or deselect) Auto-Height Overrides Height
Automation.
Right-clicking Height mode to select Auto-height Overwrite Height Automation When exchanging Pro Tools HD 12.8.2 sessions or higher that use Auto-Height Overrides Height Automation with Pro Tools HD 12.8 or 12.8.1 systems, be sure to Coalesce Pan Guide Automation first so that Height Pan Automations plays back correctly.
Coalesce Pan Guide Automation When using the Auto-Height Overrides Height Automation option to automatically generate Coalesce Pan Guide Automation Height automation, you can commit that automation using the command. Committed Height automation is calculated by interpolating Front, Rear, and Front/Rear pan automation, and the selected Auto-Height mode.
To automatically generate Height automation from Front, Rear, and Front/Rear pan automation, and the selected Auto-Height mode for one or more tracks:
1. Select the tracks for which you want to generate Height automation.
2. Choose Edit > Automation > Coalesce Pan Guide Automation.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Duplicating Object Track Pan Automation and Dolby Atmos Panner Plugin Automation When exchanging sessions between Pro Tools 12.8 or later and earlier versions of Pro Tools (such as Pro Tools 12.7.1), you probably want to ensure that object tracks have duplicate automation for track panning (Pro Tools 12.8) and Dolby Atmos panner or the Dolby Atmos Music Panner (version 1.2 or later and Pro Tools 2022.4 or later) plugin automation.
When opening an older session in Pro Tools 12.8 or later, you may prefer to use pan automation for object tracks rather than Dolby Atmos panner plugin automation.
To duplicate Dolby Atmos or Dolby Atmos Music panner plugin automation to track pan automation:
1. Select the object track (or tracks) with the Dolby Atmos panner plugin automation you want to
duplicate.
Edit > Automation > Duplicate Dolby Atmos Plugin automation to track
2. Choose
pan automation.
All Dolby Atmos and Dolby Atmos Music panner plugin automation is duplicated as track pan automation.
Both copies of the automation remain the same until you change the automation of one or the other. If you remove the Dolby Atmos panner plugin from an object track, its automation is lost.
When opening a Pro Tools 12.8 (or later) session that uses pan automation to generate Dolby Atmos metadata in a lower version of Pro Tools (such as Pro Tools 12.7.1), the pan automation is lost. Duplicate the pan automation for object tracks to Dolby Atmos panner plugin automation to ensure that these tracks playback as objects when opened in the lower version of Pro Tools.
To duplicate track pan automation to Dolby Atmos panner plugin automation:
1. Select the object track (or tracks) with pan automation you want to duplicate.
2. Ensure that the Dolby Atmos panner plugin is inserted on the track,.
3. Choose Edit > Automation > Duplicate track pan automation to Dolby Atmos
Plugin automation.
All track pan automation is duplicated as Dolby Atmos panner plugin automation. Both copies of the automation remain and remain the same until you change the automation of one or the other. If you remove the Dolby Atmos panner plugin from the track, its automation is lost.
Object Controls Mix Attribute for Grouped Tracks You can enable Object Controls in the Globals tab of the Groups dialog to link Object Control mode and Object/Bus toggle for all object tracks in the Group.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Object Controls option in Groups > Globals 60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos The Dolby Atmos Renderer window provides controls and views for the internal renderer.
g The Dolby Atmos Renderer window in Pro Tools very much resembles the main window of the Dolby Atmos application.
To show (or hide) the Dolby Atmos Renderer window, do one of the following:
- Window > Dolby Atmos Renderer.
Select (or deselect)
- Press Command+Control+= (Mac) or Control+Start+= (Windows).
Monitoring Controls Select the desired Monitoring format (monitoring paths are defined in the I/O Setup). Channel formats that are equal to or smaller than the monitor format specified in the I/O Setup are available as monitoring options.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Monitoring Available monitoring options based on your chosen Monitor Path Available Monitoring 9.1.6 7.1.4 7.1 5.1.4 5.1.2 5.1 2.0 2.0 Binaural Direct Monitor 9.1.6 x x x x x x x x x Path 9.1.4 – x x x x x x x x 7.1.6 – x x x x x x x x 7.1.4 x x x x x x x x 7.1.2 – – x x x x x x x 7.1 – – x x x x x x x 5.1.4 – – – x x x x x x 5.1.2 – – – – x x x x x 5.1 – – – – – x x x x Stereo – – – – – – x x x I/O Button Click the button to open the I/O Setup (see "Bed/Object Groups" on page 1665).
I/O Trim and Downmix Button Click the Trim & Downmix button to open the Trim and Downmix dialog (see "Trim and Downmix" on page 1701).
Mute Buttons Mutes affect stock live re-renders but do not affect custom live re-renders. Note that mutes are not reflected on any tracks, but the Mute indicator (M) lights in the Edit Window Toolbar.
Mute Click to mute (or unmute) all renderer channels (Beds and Objects).
Beds Click to mute (or unmute) all Beds.
Objects Click to mute (or unmute) all Objects.
Input Status Indicators The input status section represents all available 128 channels and how the channel IDs are assigned to beds and objects. IDs that are assigned to objects show if a track has an active connection to the object with the Object Control Mode set to Master. Beds are represented by a band.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Signal can be monitored for any connected objects and beds using peak metering. Signal starts registering at –90 dB and is color coded by decibel level.
Decibel Level Color –90 dB Dark Green –20 dB Light Green –6 dB Yellow 0 dB Red 60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Objects View View The Dolby Atmos Renderer window provides multiple views for the visualization of Object movement in a three dimensional listening environment. To help you visualize the mix in the intended listening environment, there are two display modes: Theater and Person. These display modes are purely aesthetic and do not affect the sound in anyway.
Theater Theater view is intended for mixing sound to picture. It offers a screen and lines to represent the seating rake of a theater. Objects are displayed between the bottom and the top of the screen, to help visualize where they are placed in the listener's environment.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Person Person view is intended for mixing music or in a binaural headphone environment. Objects at zero height appear at ear level of the listener.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Show Options None Object orbs are displayed with no identifier.
Object ID Object orbs are displayed with the Object ID.
Group Name Object orbs are displayed with the assigned Group Name (if any).
Binaural Mode Object orbs are displayed with the assigned Binaural mode (Off, Near, Mid, or Far).
Path Name Object orbs are displayed with the Path Name from the page in the I/O Setup.
View Modes There are three different view modes, Free, Top, and Rear. These modes help you inspect how the objects are moving amongst each other in a 3D space, from different angles. Each mode is optimized for a specific purpose.
Free Lets you freely rotate the room by clicking and dragging.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Option-click (Mac) or Alt-click (Windows) the window in any mode to return to Free mode in the default position.
Top Shows a top-down view of the space, offering more depth than when rotating the room manually.
g Double-click the room to toggle between the Top view and Free view in the last rotated position.
Rear Shows a rear view of the space, offering more depth than when rotating the room manually.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Full screen Enable Full Screen mode to hide the Input Status indicators and expand Objects view.
Room Configuration Display The Room Configuration display shows the location of speakers in the room for the selected monitoring configuration. The metering and clip-light indicators follow the ballistics of the larger output meters.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Output Meters The Output meter provide multichannel metering for the selected monitoring configuration. The metering ballistics follow the Master Meters setting (Setup > Preferences > Meters). Setting the Master Meters to PPM Digital with a 1.5 sec decay closely mirrors the ballistics of the external Dolby Atmos Renderer application (see "Metering Preferences" on page 163).
Clip Light behavior follows the Clip Indication setting and may be cleared by clicking on it.
b To clear all indicators in the application, press Option+C (Mac) or Alt+C (Windows).
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Meter order can be changed between Film and Dolby order. Right-click the meters and select the desired channel order for metering. The Dolby order follows the same order as the output meters in the Dolby Atmos Renderer application.
9.1.6 Examples:
= L C R Lw Rw Lss Rss Lsr Rsr LFE Ltf Rtf Ltm Rtm Ltr Rtr
- Film
= L R C LFE Lw Rw Lss Rss Lsr Rsr Ltf Ltm Ltr Rtm Rtf Rtr
- Dolby
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos 9.1.4 Examples:
Film= L, C, R, Lw, Rw, Lss, Rss, Lsr, Rsr, LFE, Ltf, Rtf, Ltr, Rtr
- Dolby= L, R, C, LFE, Lw, Rw, Lss, Rss, Lsr, Rsr, Ltf, Ltr, Rtf, Rtr
- 7.1.2 Examples:
Film
- = L, C, R, Lss, Rss, Lsr, Rsr, LFE, Ltm, Rtm
Dolby
- = L, R, C, LFE, Lss, Rss, Lsr, Rsr, Ltm, Ltm
You can access Dolby Atmos Settings from the Dolby Atmos Renderer window.
Click the gear icon button to open a pane to the left of the window where you can access the following settings:
- Headphone Re-Render (drop-down)
- Loudness Re-Render (drop-down)
- Custom Re-Render (button)
- I/O Setup (button)
- Trim & Downmix (button, opens the Trim and Downmix window)
- Groups (button)
Trim and Downmix Mismatch The Trim and Downmix button in the Settings pane and also in the I/O Setup lights orange if there is a delta between tabs in the window. Since this button is not visible 100% of the time, the Settings button (gear icon) lights orange accordingly if the pane is closed.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Solo and Mute Speakers Pro Tools lets you solo or mute speakers in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window.
To solo or mute speakers in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window:
1. Move the mouse cursor over a speaker icon to show solo and mute buttons.
2. Click the solo or mute button (it latches, and persists, even if you change monitoring formats).
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos L C R speakers soled in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window Suround speakers muted in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window To solo or mute an entire horizontal row, such as L/C/R or Lsr/Rrs:
- Control-click (macOS) or Start-click (Windows) any speaker in the row.
To solo or mute an entire plane:
- Option-click (macOS) or Alt-click (Windows) any speaker on either the floor or ceiling.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Solo and Mute Dolby Atmos Groups The Dolby Atmos Renderer window lets you solo and mute Dolby Atmos Groups. Groups appear at the top of the Dolby Atmos Renderer window if they are assigned to an active track in the session.
To solo any Group in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window:
- Click the Group name to solo the group Click it again to unsolo The button is yellow when the group
is soloed.
The Solo Status button at the top of the Edit window lights if any Groups are soloed in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window. You can click the Solo Status button to clear all solos.
b Control-click (Mac) or Start-click (Windows) any Group to act as a reverse-mode-clutch for X-OR and Latch. If in Latch mode, it results in an X-OR solo. If in X-OR mode, it results in a Latch solo.
b Option-click (Mac) or Alt-click (Windows) any Group to enable or disable solo for all Groups. Any muted Groups remain muted.
Soloed and Muted Groups indicated in the Edit window To mute any Group in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window:
- Command-click (Mac) or Control-click (Windows) to mute the group. Command-click (Mac) or
Control-click (Windows) it again to unsolo. The button is orange when the group is muted.
Mute status is indicated by the M button at the top of the Edit window. If any Groups are muted in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window the M button lights.
b Command-Option-click (Mac) or Control-Alt-click (Windows) any Group to enable or disable mute for all Groups.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Trim and Downmix Click the button (in the Dolby Atmos page of the I/O Setup or in the Dolby Atmos Renderer Trim & Downmix window) to open the Trim and Downmix dialog. Trim and Downmix controls determine how the surrounds and heights are processed for different mixing formats. These settings apply to monitoring, offline and live re-renders, and the settings are embedded as metadata in any bounced ADM files.
To open the Trim and Downmix dialog:
1. Do one of the following:
- Choose Setup > I/O, click the Dolby Atmos tab.
- Choose Window > Dolby Atmos Renderer.
2. Click the Trim and Downmix button.
Trim and Downmix dialog Presets Pro Tools lets you store and recall different configurations of the Trim and Downmix dialog as presets.
The Presets controls work much like the Presets controls for Batch Renaming, Fades, and Clip Effects.
Presets Buttons The Track Trim and Downmix dialog provides five Presets buttons that let you store and recall your most commonly used configurations.
You can also save and recall settings using the Librarian menu.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos To store a preset:
1. Configure the dialog as you like.
2. Command-click (Mac) or Control-click (Windows) the Presets button where you want to store the
current settings.
To recall a preset:
- Click the Presets button that contains the settings you want to recall.
Librarian Menu Use the Librarian menu to save and recall presets files (.ptpreset). These are usually saved to and recalled from the Pro Tools Presets folder (/Documents/Pro Tools/Pro Tools Presets/). However, you can save to and recall from any directory location on your system. Preset files can be shared and used on any other Pro Tools system as well.
The Librarian menu commands include:
<factory default> Restores the default settings.
Save Settings Saves the current settings. This command overwrites any previous version of the preset.
b Press Command+Shift+S (Mac) or Control+ Shift+S (Windows) to save the current settings.
Save Settings As Saves the current settings as a new preset. You can name the preset and save it to any location on your system.
Import Settings Opens the Choose a Settings File dialog focused on the default folder. Settings files stored in this location are listed by name under <factory default> in the Librarian menu. In the Choose a Settings File dialog, you can navigate to any location on your system to select and import the Settings preset file you want.
Delete Current Settings File Permanently deletes the current settings file from disk.
Lock (Unlock) Settings File Locks (or unlocks) the selected Settings file so that it cannot (or can) be edited.
New Session Defaults/Current Session The Trim and Downmix Window is divided up into two tabs, New Session Defaults and Current Session.
Because this window affects the sound of the session, it is important that the settings travel with the session. However, it is also important that opening a session does not affect your own personal defaults.
New Session Defaults Settings in this tab determine the settings for any newly created session. They do not affect the sound of the current session.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Current Session Settings in this tab affect the current session. If a setting differs between the currently open session and the New Session Defaults tab, settings that differ light orange to help you identify them. If there are differences, the Apply button at the bottom of each tab also lights orange. This button—Apply Current (on the New Session Defaults pane)/Apply Defaults(on the Current Session New Session Session pane)—lets you to pull over the settings from the opposite tab.
Trim and Downmix settings mismatched g The Trim and Downmix buttons in the Dolby Atmos Renderer window and in the I/O Setup also light orange to warn you when you open a session that contains settings which are different from your defaults.
Apply Current Session/Apply New Session Defaults Button Click Apply Current Session to use the current session settings for new session defaults. Click Apply New to use the new session settings for the current session.
Session Defaults Downmix Target downmix format: 2.0 Direct, 5.1 and 2.0, 5.1.2, 5.1.4, and 7.1.
Auto Enable to automatically set the and settings for the target downmix format. Disable this Trims Balance option to set the Trims and Balance settings for the target downmix format manually.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Trims Surround Set a dB trim for the surround content when it is folded into the mix.
Height Set a dB trim for the overhead content when it is folded into the mix.
Balance Overhead Specify where the overhead content folds down toward the front or the rear of the soundscape.
Listener Plane Specify where surround content folds down toward the front or the rear of the soundscape.
Downmix Control 5.1/5.1.x Lo/Ro Default Downmixes to 7.1 and then to 5.1 using the following coefficients:
LS = 0 dB x Lss + 0 dB x Lrs
- Rs = 0 dB x Rss + 0 dB x Rrs
- Dolby Pro Logic IIx
Downmixes to 7.1 and then to 5.1 using the following coefficients:
Ls = Lss + (–1.2 dB x Lrs) + (–6.2 dB x Rrs)
- Ls = Lss + (–6.2 dB x Lrs) + (–1.2 dB x Rrs)
- Direct Render with Room Balance
Downmixes from Dolby Atmos to 5.1 (without downmixing from 7.1) by applying an updated Dolby rendering algorithm that reduces the comb filter effects associated with phantom imaging of objects positioned halfway between the front and rear of the room. Room balance refers to how the renderer deals with content that is panned between the midpoint and rear of the room. With this setting, the content is presented at a constant level in the surround speakers between the rear and midpoint of the room, avoiding any need for phantom imaging until it is in the front half of the room.
Direct Render Downmixes from Dolby Atmos to 5.1 (without downmixing via 7.1) by using the rendering engine in the Dolby Atmos renderer to accurately re-create the sound field at the central listening position using phantom imaging between the surround speakers and front speakers.
2.0 Lo/Ro Default Downmixes to 2.0 using the following coefficients:
- LS = 0 dB x Lss + 0 dB x Lrs
- Rs = 0 dB x Rss + 0 dB x Rrs
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Downmixes to 2.0 using the following coefficients:
Lt = L + (–3 dB × C) – (–1.2 dB × Ls) – (–6.2 dB × Rs)
- Rt = R + (–3 dB × C) + (–6.2 dB × Ls) + (–1.2 dB × Rs)
- g
Stereo direct is not downmixed using an additional downmix layout. As a result the front/back balance of stereo direct trims does not affect the output signal when an object is at maximum height, or if the surround and height trims are set to zero.
2.0 Direct Traditional 2.0 mixes are first mixed to either 7.1 to 5.1 and then to 2.0, or to 5.1 to 2.0 (depending on your downmix settings). Stereo Direct creates a 2.0 mix without any extra down-mixing from larger formats.
While stereo Direct Trim settings are included in the metadata for BWF ADM files, most decoders currently on the market use the traditional 2.0 settings. Stereo Direct can be used for monitoring or for generating re- renders.
Import Trim and Downmix Settings You can import Trim and Downmix settings from either sessions or ADM files using Data.
Import Session g The Trim and Downmix Window is only available when using the internal renderer, however, it is still possible to import trim and downmix settings into the Pro Tools session when using an external renderer. Settings are stored so that they are available in the case that internal mode is used later.
To import Trim and Downmix settings from a session or ADM file:
File > Import > Session Data.
1. Choose
2. Navigate to the location of the session or ADM file you want, select it, and click Open.
3. Select the option under
Import Session Data, Dolby Atmos Trim and Downmix option enabled
4. Click OK.
Using a Master Fader to Control Internal Renderer Monitoring You can create a Master Fader track that matches the width of your Monitor Path. This is the final output stage before the main monitoring output of the renderer reaches the speakers. This Master Fader is useful for volume attenuation of the renderer output, room tuning plugins, or any final limiting. Plugins on the Master Fader are included when you bounce the main monitor output.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos g You can bounce the main output while the renderer is processing.
Assigning a Master Fader to the Renderer Monitor output If the Master Fader is assigned in a session, and that session is opened on a system with a different width monitor path, and plugins are present on the Master Fader, Pro Tools prompts you to change the width of the Master Fader. If no plugins are present, Pro Tools automatically changes the width.
Binaural Render Mode Binaural Render mode lets you apply distance model metadata to each of the object or bed channels in your mix. This additional processing is only applied to monitoring and re-renders when their respective formats are set as binaural. The various Binaural distance model settings (Off, Near, Mid, and Far) apply varying levels of spatialization to each of the bed or object channels. Consider these settings as the amount of virtualized distance between an object or bed and the listener’s head. Binaural Render Mode metadata is written to the entire ADM export, from beginning to end. It is not possible to automate this setting.
Binaural output is not designed for playback over speakers.
The binaural metadata is applied to the headphone output during monitoring, recording, or playback of a master. This metadata is not included with speaker processing. For more information, visit Dolby’s FAQ online.
Binaural monitoring requires additional CPU processing, so if your session is nearing the limits of your CPU, you may want to change your Monitoring and Live Headphone Re-render from Binaural to 2.0.
The Binaural format only works with the main monitoring output and the stock headphone re-render at higher buffer sizes. The following table lists the HW buffer size requirements for Pro Tools systems at 48 kHz and 96 kHz.
Pro Tools System Sample Rate HW Buffer Size Non-HDX 48 kHz 512 and higher 96 kHz 1024 and higher HDX Hybrid Engine 48 kHz 1024 (only) 96 kHz 2048 (only) g Custom binaural re-renders are supported at all buffer sizes.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos In a typical Dolby Atmos mix, two or more Pro Tools | HDX systems provide playback of tracks and stems. An additional Pro Tools | HDX system is configured as the recorder (dubber). The recorder also communicates with the Dolby Atmos renderer.
By linking Object and audio recording on the Pro Tools recorder system, audio and metadata punch in/out occur simultaneously on the Pro Tools recorder.
Metadata Only for Object panning in the Dolby Atmos renderer (or rendering and mastering workstation). This Object.
includes Pro Tools tracks with their Object Toggle set to Pro Tools Pan Automation Bus).
Only for Bus panning in Pro Tools (tracks with Object Toggle set to The following section is an example workflow of how to configure and operate Pro Tools and a Dolby Atmos renderer.
Configuring the Pro Tools Recorder To configure the Pro Tools recorder system:
1. Choose Setup > I/O, click to display the Dolby Atmos tab, then do the following:
- If necessary, establish communication with the Dolby Atmos renderer (see "Enabling the
External Renderer" on page 1657).
- Click OK to close the Peripherals dialog.
2. Recommended: Choose Setup > Preferences, click to display the Operation tab, then do the
following:
- Select Enable Automation in Record.
- Select PEC/Direct Style Input Monitoring.
g When PEC/Direct Style Input Monitoring is enabled, the Pro Tools Input Monitor switch label P D lights green and shows (for PEC, playing back from disk), or is unlit and shows (for Direct, (Input) either lit green when Input mode is active, or unlit when playing back from disk.
3. Click to display the Mixing tab and disable Link Object and Audio Recording only if you do
not want to record object metadata along with object audio.
OK
4. Click to close the Preferences dialog.
5. Enable DestructivePunch, TrackPunch, or QuickPunch. (For details, see "Punch Recording Modes" on
page 807) g If you are tracking with DSP hardware, you might want to re-assign a track directly to the main output while overdubbing, so you can maintain DSP low latency. The signal will not be spatialized, but you can assign it back to the renderer when you are done. This lets you use a high buffer size while keeping low latency for the overdub.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos To configure tracks and mix from a playback system to a recording system:
1. On each object track on the recording system, set the Object Control mode to Record.
You can set the Object Control mode by clicking the Object Control button shown in the Track Output window, or in the Mix and Edit windows when showing Object view. If your system includes an S6 with a Master Post Module, you can use its Record arm switch.
2. Enable the desired Automation mode for each track (such as Touch, Latch, or Touch/Latch).
3. On-screen, the pan dot turns red indicating the track is in an automation write mode.
4. Record enable all tracks (if using DestructivePunch, be sure to Prepare DPE Tracks if necessary).
5. On the playback system, set Object Control Mode to Master for the corresponding object.
6. Begin playback and adjust parameters, toggling monitor source as needed. Toggle the monitor
source on the recorder as needed.
7. When ready, punch into the recorder system to record pan metadata and audio from the player to
the recorder. (Typically, using Destructive Punch mode.) You will most commonly use Satellite to synchronize the playback and recording systems (see "Satellite Link" on page 1889).
Importing Bed/Object-Related Track Data from Another Session When importing session data from another Dolby Atmos session, you can import the bed and object output path assignments (if any) as well as Bus and object toggle setting for tracks selected for import. This is vital when importing tracks already defined as objects from another session.
To import bed and object tracks from another session:
File > Import > Session Data.
1. Choose
Open.
2. Navigate to the session from which you want to import object tracks, select it and click
3. In the Tracks section of the Import Session Data dialog, identify the tracks you want to import
(Source) and specify the Destination for each track (either create a new object track or replace an existing object track).
4. In the Track Data to Import dialog, ensure that the Bed/Object Assignment and Toggle
Setting option is enabled (this option is selected by default).
5. To include Group assignments for each track, ensure that the Bed/Object Group Assignment
option is enabled (this option is selected by default).
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos
6. Configure the rest of the Import Session Data dialog as desired and click OK.
Track Data to Import, Bed/Object Assignment and Toggle Setting and Bed/Object Group Assignment options enabled 60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Importing ADM BWF Files A Dolby Atmos Master File in Broadcast Wave format is a .wav file (ADM BWF) that includes audio and audio and metadata converted to pan automation.
After creating a Dolby Atmos master file set with the Dolby Atmos renderer, you can use the Dolby Atmos Conversion Tool to convert the Dolby Atmos master to a .wav (ADM BWF) file, export it directly from the Bounce Mix.
Pro Tools also lets you link to an ADM file when importing session data. This makes for much faster session data import since Pro Tools does not need to convert the ADM into multi-mono files.
g Linking to a WAV ADM BWF file is useful for quickly opening a file for inspection, or when basic edits need to be performed before re-exporting. This is quicker than creating new media upon import, and it does not take up additional disk space. However, linked ADM files are more demanding on CPU and disk usage. It is recommended that you use a fast drive, such as an SSD to mitigate these demands. If you encounter any significant performance issues, use the Force to target session format option instead.
Performance decreases significantly if any clips linked to an ADM are shifted from their locations relative to one another. In other words, you can move the entire ADM around on the timeline, but the system load will be much lighter if you leave the clips lined up at the same start time.
To import an ADM file into Pro Tools:
1. Create a new session.
2. Choose File > Import > Session Data.
3. Navigate to and select the .wav (ADM BWF) file you want to import.
4. Click Open.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Import Session Data from an ADM file
5. In the Import Session Data dialog, select a Destination for the Source tracks in the .wav (ADM BWF)
file.
Import Session Data, custom data for ADM files
6. Select what Custom Data to import:
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos
  - Bed/Object Output Assignment
Imports bed and object output path assignments for imported tracks.
  - Object Pan Data
Imports Dolby Atmos metadata and converts it to track pan automation.
  - Add Bed/Object Group Name to I/O Setup
Lets you add the bed or object group name to the Output Bus path in the I/O Setup for each imported track. This option is only available if Pro Tools is currently set to manage Bed/Object Groups.
  - Add Bed/Object Group Name to Track Name
Lets you add the bed or object group name to the Beginning or the End of the track name.
  - Import – Replace Existing Playlists
Imports the media and replaces the Main playlist media and panning data.
  - Import – Overlay New On Existing Playlists
Imports the media and overlays media and panning data on existing playlists.
  - Do Not Import
Lets you to import panning data without the media.
7. Configure any other Import Session Data settings as desired.
8. Click OK.
You can import audio from .wav (ADM BWF) files using the Import Audio command or by dragging and dropping the file into a session. However, only the audio is imported, and bed and channel order may be lost.
> **Note:** For more information about Dolby Atmos Master files, contact Dolby.
ADM File Metadata on Import Pro Tools shows the following information in the Import Session Data dialog when importing an ADM file:
Name
- Type
- Start Time
- End Time
- FFOA (First Frame of Action)
- Timecode Format
- Bed Configurations
- Number of Beds
- Number of Objects
- Audio Sample Rate
- Audio Bit Depth
- Application and version that created the ADM
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Opening Legacy Dolby Atmos Sessions When a Pro Tools 2023.9 (or earlier) Atmos session (configured with buses mapped to beds and objects) is opened in Pro Tools 2023.12 (or later), the buses move to the Dolby Atmos page, where you can choose to use an external Dolby Atmos Renderer application or the internal renderer.
If you used Pro Tools 2023.9 (or earlier) to feed an output-mapped-bus to an external renderer, but that bus was not mapped to a bed, the bus does not automatically move to the Dolby Atmos tab—therefore it cannot work with the internal renderer or be included in an ADM or re-render bounce. However, this can be corrected by right-clicking on the bus and choosing Move to Dolby Atmos tab as a Bed.
Export ADM files Pro Tools lets you export to the ADM BWF file format with Dolby Atmos content. ADM BWF files can include audio and Dolby Atmos metadata from the Dolby Atmos renderer or a Dolby Atmos master.
This facilitates quick export of Dolby Atmos mixes using Offline bounce directly from Pro Tools. You can generate new ADM files from a new session with new metadata, or rework previous ADM files, passing along or changing the previous metadata accordingly.
An ADM file bounced from Pro Tools includes all channels (beds and objects) active in the session that are mapped to the Dolby Atmos renderer.
To export Dolby Atmos ADM from a Pro Tools session:
1. Choose File > Bounce Mix.
2. In the Bounce Mix dialog, type the File Name.
3. Select WAV (Dolby Atmos) as the File Type.
4. Select the desired Sample Rate for the export in the Audio section.
5. Configure the Dolby Atmos section of the Bounce Mix dialog according to your delivery requirements
(see "Dolby Atmos Bounce Mix Options" below).
6. Enable or disable Offline for offline or real-time bounce.
7. Click Bounce.
g Pro Tools thins automation in exported Dolby Atmos BWF WAV ADM files for any time range that does not contain any audio content. This can dramatically reduce the size of an ADM file, particularly when there is a large panning automation ramp between clips in different areas of the timeline.
The Dolby Atmos pane of the Bounce Mix dialog displays the number of Beds and Objects to be included in the bounced ADM file. These are defined by the Bed and Object buses that are mapped to the Dolby Atmos renderer. Only Bed and Object buses that are mapped to the Dolby Atmos Renderer are included in the bounced ADM file. All unmapped paths are excluded. You can also embed program-level metadata, Bed and Object Group assignments, FFOA (First Frame of Action) timecode location, and other Dolby Atmos renderer settings.
When using any of these options, Pro Tools take the Trim and Downmix, as well as the Binaural information from the selected source. Group information and Bed/Object descriptions always come from Pro Tools.
Beds and Objects Beds and objects are included automatically in the bounced ADM file if the following criteria are met:
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos They must be mapped to a bed or object to the Dolby Atmos renderer in the Dolby Atmos pane of
- the I/O Setup.
- The beds and objects must be actively used in the session.
An ADM file bounced from Pro Tools includes all channels (beds and objects) active in the session that are mapped to the Dolby Atmos renderer. The Dolby Atmos page of the I/O Setup lets you map beds in a similar way to that of objects. Once these mappings are made for the active beds and objects in the session, the number of mapped Beds and the number of mapped Objects are displayed in the Bounce Mix dialog.
Audio from any object-assigned tracks not set to Master is included in the bounced ADM BWF file as long as another active track routed to the same object is set to Master. However, any pan data from those tracks is excluded.
Beds Displays the number of Beds mapped to the Dolby Atmos renderer in the Dolby Atmos pane of the I/O Setup. Beds are bounced at the output point in the signal chain. Bouncing at the output point ensures that all audio being sent to the Dolby Atmos renderer is included in the bounce.
Objects Displays the number of Objects mapped to the Dolby Atmos renderer in the Dolby Atmos pane of the I/O Setup. Each object is bounced as a unique mono object channel.
g Object pan metadata for a track is only included in an exported BWF ADM file if Object Control Mode is set to Master for that track.
I/O Setup Button Click the I/O Setup button to open the I/O Setup and validate Bed and Object mappings before bouncing to an ADM file. If necessary, configure the Dolby Atmos page of I/O Setup for the ADM bounce. Be sure that all Beds and Objects that you want to include in the bounce are mapped to the Dolby Atmos renderer.
? Button Click the ? button for information about beds and objects to be bounced. For a detailed report of what will be included in the bounce, click the Report button in the Info dialog (or click OK to close it). The resulting Included Dolby Atmos Renderer Mappings dialog lists the beds and objects to be included in the ADM WAV export. For beds, the assigned output name is listed. For objects, the assigned object name is listed.
Additionally, associated Dolby Atmos Renderer inputs are listed as well as Bed/Object Groups (if any are assigned).
Add FFOA FFOA (First Frame of Action) is an optional piece of metadata used to define where the content starts on the Pro Tools Timeline. Check your content delivery specifications to see if this is a required piece of metadata for the ADM file you need to deliver or not. If so, be sure to enable the Add FFOA option. In the Bounce Mix window, the FFOA timecode location defaults to the nearest hour that is equal to or later than the start of the timeline selection in the session. Entering a timecode location that is before the timeline selection start is disallowed.
Frame Rate The Frame Rate specified in the Session Setup is displayed in the Bounce Mix dialog for reference only and it cannot be changed. Note that drop fame rates higher than 30 fps are not supported. Frame Rates larger than 30 fps are shown as their SD derivative, and SD derivative will be embedded in the bounced ADM. For example, a 48 fps session displays 24 fps in this field.
60 Mixing to Dolby Atmos Sample Rate 48 kHz and 96 kHz are supported for export.
Embed Program-Level Metadata Program-level metadata refers to metadata that is not time variable and applies to the ADM BWF master as a whole. It is not part of the dynamic object metadata. It consists of static settings, often used to affect the resulting sound of the mix, as well as the final Dolby encoding process. Program-level metadata is fixed for the entire session.
Program level metadata consists of the following:
Downmix
- 5.1 downmix
- 5.1 to 2.0 downmix
- Trim metadata
- Front/back balance
- Binaural render mode metadata
- Using the Internal renderer, only the Connected Renderer Settings option is available. When using an
External renderer, there are three different options for embedding program-level metadata:
Factory Default Uses the factory default settings of the Dolby Atmos renderer to for any applicable program-level metadata.
Connected Renderer Uses the connected Dolby Atmos renderer setting as metadata for the bounced ADM BWF file. This option is only available if the Dolby Atmos renderer is connected with Pro Tools.
Previous ADM File Uses the metadata from the last ADM file imported into the Pro Tools session. If no ADM has been imported to the current session, the most recent import since launch will be available. The name of the last imported ADM file is displayed to the right of this option in the Embed Program-Level Metadata selector for reference.
If more than one ADM file has been imported into the session, only the metadata from the latest ADM file is maintained.
